network.overall_strategy: L-Proline (C5H9NO2) is a cyclic amino acid whose prebiotic synthesis requires assembly of a pyrrolidine ring from smaller precursors. The network pursues three complementary strategies grounded in literature: (1) A Mannich-type route from glycine + 3 formaldehyde + reductant (Sources 2595), requiring proper C2+3C1=C5 stoichiometry with H2 as reductant; (2) A biological-analog route via α-ketoglutarate → glutamate → glutamate-5-semialdehyde (GSA) → P5C → proline, where α-KG is formed by aldol condensation of pyruvate (C3) + glyoxylate (C2) following the HKG cycle (Sources 10738); and (3) A proline aminonitrile route via Δ1-pyrroline + HCN (Sources 22185), where Δ1-pyrroline derives from succinaldehyde + NH3, and succinaldehyde is formed by Fe0-catalyzed reduction of α-KG decarboxylation product succinic semialdehyde. Hub molecules (formaldehyde, glycine, pyruvate, α-ketoglutarate, P5C) create convergence points linking hydrothermal CO2 fixation, surface Strecker/Mannich chemistry, and biochemical cyclization/reduction steps.

reactions.name: CO2 hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich fluids

reactions.mechanism: CO2 adsorbs on the greigite surface. H2 dissociates on FeNiS sites. Hydride transfer to CO2 produces formate (HCOO-). This mirrors the first step of the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway catalyzed by formate dehydrogenase, whose active site resembles greigite.
reactions.reasoning: CO2 reduction to formate on iron-sulfur minerals is one of the best-characterized prebiotic carbon fixation reactions. Greigite (Fe3S4) is a structural analog of biological [Fe4S4] clusters and catalyzes this reaction under hydrothermal conditions (Roldan et al., 2015; Herschy et al., 2014). This is the entry point for all carbon into the network. Sources 9400 describe Fe2+-catalyzed reactions producing key metabolic precursors at 70°C.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on magnetite

conditions.other: Subseafloor hydrothermal, rock-water interactions, strongly reducing

reactions.mechanism: Formate is reduced to formaldehyde via 2-electron reduction. Fe0 acts as the electron donor, being oxidized to Fe2+. Magnetite surface provides the catalytic platform for H-transfer. HCOO- + 2[H] → CH2O + H2O.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type reduction of formate to formaldehyde on magnetite surfaces has been demonstrated under hydrothermal conditions (McCollom et al., 1999; He et al., 2019). Fe0 provides the thermodynamic driving force. Formaldehyde is an essential C1 building block. Sources 2595 require formaldehyde concentrations of ~30 mM for amino acid synthesis.

reactions.name: Formate carboxylation to glyoxylate on iron minerals

conditions.other: Alkaline vent, CO2-rich fluids mixing with H2-rich fluids

reactions.mechanism: Formate undergoes carboxylation via CO2 insertion on the pyrite surface. The activated C1 formate attacks CO2 to form the C2 α-keto acid glyoxylate (OHCCOOH). FeS clusters provide electron density to activate CO2.
reactions.reasoning: Glyoxylate is a key C2 α-keto acid required for the HKG cycle (Muchowska et al., 2017; Sources 10738). Pyrite-mediated carboxylation reactions have been proposed in the iron-sulfur world hypothesis (Wachtershauser, 1988). CO2 fixation into C-C bonds on metal sulfide surfaces is a core feature of hydrothermal prebiotic chemistry.

reactions.name: CO2 reductive fixation to pyruvate on greigite/magnetite

conditions.other: 3 CO2 + 4 H2 → pyruvate + 3 H2O; alkaline vent, native iron present

reactions.mechanism: Sequential CO2 reduction and C-C bond formation on greigite/magnetite surfaces. CO2 → formate → CO (or formyl intermediate) → acetyl intermediate → pyruvate via further CO2 insertion. Each step involves H2-driven reduction on the mineral surface. Net: 3CO2 + 4H2 → CH3COCOOH + 3H2O.
reactions.reasoning: Direct CO2-to-pyruvate synthesis on iron minerals has been demonstrated by Varma et al. (2018) and Muchowska et al. (2019). Greigite provides the catalytic surface mimicking the acetyl-CoA pathway. This is a cornerstone of the 'metabolism-first' hypothesis. Sources 9400 describe Fe2+-catalyzed formation of metabolic precursors including pyruvate.

reactions.name: Formate to acetate on greigite (acetyl-CoA pathway analog)

conditions.other: 2 HCOO- + 2 H2 → CH3COO- + 2 H2O; Wood-Ljungdahl analog

reactions.mechanism: Two formate molecules undergo reductive coupling on greigite surface. First formate is reduced to a methyl group, second is reduced to CO, then carbonyl insertion forms the acetyl group which is hydrolyzed to acetate. 2HCOO- + 2H2 → CH3COO- + OH- + H2O.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate synthesis from CO2/formate on FeNiS minerals mimics the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway and has been demonstrated under hydrothermal conditions (Preiner et al., 2020; Herschy et al., 2014). This provides an alternative C2 building block feeding into pyruvate synthesis.

reactions.name: Acetate carboxylation to pyruvate on pyrite

conditions.other: CH3COO- + CO2 + 2[H] → CH3COCOO- + H2O; reductive carboxylation

reactions.mechanism: Acetate undergoes reductive carboxylation at the methyl C-H bond. CO2 inserts via a metal-carboxylate intermediate on the pyrite surface. Fe0 provides electrons for the reductive activation. Product is pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive carboxylation of acetate to pyruvate on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces has been proposed as a key step in the reverse/reductive TCA cycle analog (Wachtershauser, 1990; Muchowska et al., 2019). Sources 9400 report Fe2+-catalyzed formation of pyruvate-related intermediates.

reactions.name: α-KG decarboxylation to succinic semialdehyde

conditions.other: Thermal decarboxylation of α-keto acid; C5 → C4 + CO2

reactions.mechanism: α-Ketoglutarate (HOOC-CO-CH2-CH2-COOH) undergoes thermal decarboxylation at the C1 position adjacent to the keto group. The β-keto acid motif facilitates loss of CO2, yielding succinic semialdehyde (OHC-CH2-CH2-COOH). Pyrite surface stabilizes the transition state.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of α-keto acids is well-established in prebiotic chemistry and occurs readily at hydrothermal temperatures. This is the non-enzymatic analog of the α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase reaction. In biology, this produces succinyl-CoA; prebiotically, the product is succinic semialdehyde. The β-keto acid decarboxylation is thermodynamically favorable (Guzman & Martin, 2009).

reactions.name: Succinic semialdehyde reduction to succinaldehyde on Fe0

conditions.other: OHC-CH2-CH2-COOH + 2[H] → OHC-CH2-CH2-CHO + H2O; carboxylate reduction on native iron

reactions.mechanism: Succinic semialdehyde adsorbs on the Fe0 surface via the carboxylate group. Fe0 donates 2 electrons and H+ is provided by water. The carboxylate is reduced through a hemiacetal-like intermediate to the aldehyde. Fe0 → Fe2+ provides the thermodynamic driving force (ΔG << 0 for Fe0 oxidation). Net: RCOOH + 2[H] → RCHO + H2O.
reactions.reasoning: Carboxylate reduction to aldehyde is thermodynamically challenging in solution but becomes feasible on Fe0 surfaces due to the highly negative reduction potential of Fe0/Fe2+ (-0.44 V). Fe0 is present in hydrothermal systems from meteoritic iron and serpentinization products. Carboxylate reduction on metal surfaces under hydrothermal conditions has been discussed by Huber & Wachtershauser (2006) and is thermodynamically driven by the strong reducing capacity of native iron. This is the rate-limiting step of the proline aminonitrile pathway.

reactions.name: Formaldehyde-HCN coupling to glycolonitrile

conditions.other: Neutral to slightly basic pH; cyanohydrin formation

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of CN- to formaldehyde carbonyl. HCN deprotonates in slightly basic conditions to give CN-, which attacks CH2O to form glycolonitrile (HOCH2CN). Montmorillonite concentrates both reactants via adsorption.
reactions.reasoning: Glycolonitrile formation from formaldehyde and HCN is a well-established prebiotic reaction and is central to the cyanosulfidic scenario (Patel et al., 2015; Sutherland, 2016). Sources 9376, 16400 identify glycolonitrile as a critical precursor in prebiotic amino acid synthesis. This is one of the most robust prebiotic reactions known.

reactions.name: Glycolonitrile hydrolysis and amination to glycine

conditions.other: Aqueous solution, NH3 present; nitrile hydrolysis + aminonitrile pathway

reactions.mechanism: Glycolonitrile (HOCH2CN) undergoes aminolysis: NH3 displaces OH to form aminoacetonitrile (H2NCH2CN). Subsequent hydrolysis of the nitrile group (CN → CONH2 → COOH) yields glycine. Montmorillonite catalyzes the hydrolysis step. This is a modified Strecker pathway where the aminonitrile intermediate is formed via the glycolonitrile route.
reactions.reasoning: The glycolonitrile-to-glycine pathway via aminonitrile intermediates is well-established in prebiotic chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Sources 9376, 16400, 6942). The Strecker reaction (aldehyde + NH3 + HCN → amino acid) is one of the most robust prebiotic amino acid synthesis routes (Sources 6940, 21189). Montmorillonite facilitates hydrolysis and concentrates reactants (Sources 9431).

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of glycine from formaldehyde

conditions.other: Classical Strecker: CH2O + NH3 + HCN → aminoacetonitrile → hydrolysis → glycine

reactions.mechanism: Step 1: Formaldehyde reacts with NH3 to form the imine H2C=NH. Step 2: HCN adds to the imine to form aminoacetonitrile (H2NCH2CN). Step 3: Nitrile hydrolysis in water yields glycine. CH2O + NH3 + HCN + H2O → H2NCH2COOH + NH3 (regenerated from HCN hydrolysis byproduct).
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is one of the most well-characterized prebiotic amino acid formation reactions (Miller, 1953; Strecker, 1850; Sources 6940, 6942, 21189, 10835). It has been demonstrated under a variety of simulated prebiotic conditions including spark discharge experiments, hydrothermal fluids, and surface evaporitic pools. The direct Strecker route to glycine from CH2O is the simplest and most robust pathway.

reactions.name: Formose-type aldol to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: 2 CH2O → HOCH2CHO; base-catalyzed formose reaction first step

reactions.mechanism: Two formaldehyde molecules undergo base-catalyzed aldol condensation. The Cannizzaro disproportionation provides the initial enolizable species. Ca(OH)2 or borate minerals act as base. Borate stabilizes the glycolaldehyde product against further aldol reactions by forming borate esters. 2 CH2O → HOCH2CHO.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction producing glycolaldehyde from formaldehyde is a classic prebiotic reaction (Butlerow, 1861; Breslow, 1959). Borate minerals stabilize the sugar products and prevent tar formation (Ricardo et al., 2004; Sources reference formose reaction with borate). This provides C2 aldehyde building blocks for further synthesis.

reactions.name: Glycolaldehyde oxidation to glyoxylate

conditions.other: UV photocatalytic oxidation; HOCH2CHO → OHCCOOH + 2[H]; anoxic but UV-driven

reactions.mechanism: TiO2 absorbs UV light generating electron-hole pairs. The hole oxidizes glycolaldehyde at the alcohol group: HOCH2CHO → OHC-COOH (glyoxylate). Under anoxic conditions, Fe3+ or the TiO2 hole serves as the terminal electron acceptor. This provides a surface-environment route to glyoxylate complementing the hydrothermal carboxylation route (rxn_003).
reactions.reasoning: TiO2 photocatalytic oxidation of organic molecules under UV irradiation is well-established in prebiotic surface chemistry. Early Earth lacked an ozone layer, providing intense UV for photocatalysis. This provides a convergent route to glyoxylate (also formed hydrothermally in rxn_003), creating a key convergence point in the network.

reactions.name: Pyruvate-glyoxylate aldol to 4-hydroxy-2-ketoglutarate (HKG)

conditions.other: CH3COCOO- (C3) + OHCCOO- (C2) → HKG (C5); aldol condensation

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate (C3) and glyoxylate (C2) undergo aldol condensation. The enolizable methyl group of pyruvate attacks the aldehyde of glyoxylate. This forms 4-hydroxy-2-oxoglutarate (HKG, C5), the signature intermediate of the HKG cycle. Fe2+ or montmorillonite Lewis acid sites catalyze the aldol. Carbon balance: C3 + C2 = C5 ✓.
reactions.reasoning: This aldol condensation is the central reaction of the Hydroxy Ketoglutarate (HKG) cycle demonstrated by Muchowska et al. (2017, 2019) and described in Sources 10738, 10732, 9988. The HKG cycle provides a prebiotically plausible analog of the TCA cycle. Pyruvate + glyoxylate → HKG is one of the most robust prebiotic C-C bond forming reactions, with proper C3 + C2 = C5 stoichiometry.

reactions.name: HKG dehydration/reduction to alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: HKG → α-KG via dehydration then reduction of double bond; Fe2+ as electron source

reactions.mechanism: 4-Hydroxy-2-oxoglutarate undergoes dehydration to form 2-oxoglutaconate (elimination of H2O from the 3,4-hydroxyl). Then the resulting double bond is reduced by Fe2+-donated electrons (or H2) to yield α-ketoglutarate. Net: HKG + 2[H] → α-KG + H2O.
reactions.reasoning: The conversion of HKG to α-KG via dehydration-reduction is part of the HKG cycle (Muchowska et al., 2017, 2019; Sources 10738). Fe2+ serves as the electron donor for the reduction step, consistent with the Fe2+-catalyzed protometabolic chemistry described in Sources 9400. This provides α-KG (C5), the direct precursor to glutamate.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of α-ketoglutarate to glutamate

conditions.other: α-KG + NH3 + H2 → glutamate + H2O; reductive amination

reactions.mechanism: The C2 ketone of α-ketoglutarate (HOOC-CO-CH2-CH2-COOH) condenses with NH3 to form an imine (Schiff base). The C=N double bond is then reduced by H2 (or Fe2+/FeS-donated electrons) to give the amine, yielding glutamate. This is a standard reductive amination. Carbon count preserved: C5 → C5.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids to amino acids is one of the most fundamental prebiotic reactions (Bada, 2013; Sources 9396, 9334). The conversion of α-KG to glutamate by reductive amination with NH3 has been demonstrated under prebiotic conditions (Kitadai & Maruyama, 2018). Fe2+ facilitates the reduction. This is the biological pathway in reverse of glutamate dehydrogenase.

reactions.name: Glutamate partial reduction to glutamate-5-semialdehyde (GSA)

conditions.other: Selective reduction of C5-carboxylate to aldehyde; HOOC-CH(NH2)-CH2-CH2-COOH + 2[H] → HOOC-CH(NH2)-CH2-CH2-CHO + H2O

reactions.mechanism: The γ-carboxylate group of glutamate is selectively reduced to an aldehyde on the Fe0 surface. The α-carboxylate is less accessible due to the adjacent amino group providing steric/electronic differentiation. Fe0 donates electrons, and the reaction proceeds through a hemiacetal intermediate. This is the prebiotically most challenging step in this pathway.
reactions.reasoning: In biology, this step is performed by γ-glutamyl kinase + γ-glutamyl phosphate reductase (using ATP + NADPH). Prebiotically, Fe0 provides sufficient reducing potential (E° = -0.44 V) for carboxylate-to-aldehyde reduction. The reaction is endergonic in solution but is driven by Fe0 oxidation. Similar mineral-surface carboxylate reductions have been proposed by Huber & Wachtershauser (1997, 2006). This is explicitly acknowledged as the rate-limiting step of this pathway.

reactions.name: Spontaneous cyclization of GSA to P5C

conditions.other: Intramolecular imine formation; loss of H2O

reactions.mechanism: The free amino group at C2 of glutamate-5-semialdehyde attacks the C5 aldehyde intramolecularly, forming a 5-membered ring with an imine (C=N) bond. One equivalent of water is lost. This Schiff base cyclization is spontaneous and thermodynamically favorable due to the stability of the 5-membered ring. Product is Δ1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate (P5C).
reactions.reasoning: The spontaneous cyclization of GSA to P5C is well-documented in biochemistry and occurs non-enzymatically in aqueous solution (Adams & Frank, 1980). This is the key ring-closure step that forms the pyrrolidine skeleton of proline. The reaction has no kinetic barrier and proceeds at room temperature, making it highly prebiotically plausible.

reactions.name: Reduction of P5C to L-Proline

conditions.catalyst: Fe2+/FeS as electron donor; or borohydride analog
conditions.other: P5C + 2[H] → Proline; imine reduction

reactions.mechanism: The C=N imine bond of Δ1-pyrroline-5-carboxylate (P5C) is reduced to a C-N single bond by 2-electron reduction. H2 or Fe2+/sulfide-donated electrons provide the reducing equivalents. This converts the cyclic imine to a pyrrolidine, yielding proline. In biology this is catalyzed by P5C reductase (NADH-dependent). Prebiotically, imine reduction is thermodynamically favorable with mineral reductants.
reactions.reasoning: Imine reduction is one of the most thermodynamically favorable reductions in aqueous solution and occurs readily with mild reductants. Fe2+/H2S systems can provide the required electrons (Barge et al., 2019). The non-enzymatic reduction of P5C to proline has been discussed in the context of prebiotic amino acid synthesis. Levine et al. (2008) demonstrated enantioselective synthesis and amplification of amino acids including proline under prebiotic conditions (Sources 15388). Racemic proline is the expected prebiotic product; enantiomeric excess may arise from secondary amplification processes (Sources 21298).

reactions.name: Mannich-type cyclization of glycine with formaldehyde to proline

conditions.other: H2NCH2COOH + 3 CH2O + 2 H2 → C5H9NO2 + 3 H2O; Mannich-type sequential addition and cyclization; wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: Step 1: Glycine + CH2O → N-methyleneglycine (Schiff base, H2C=N-CH2-COOH + H2O). Step 2: Mannich addition of second CH2O to the N-methyleneglycine → N,N-bis(hydroxymethyl)glycine or equivalent 3-carbon chain on nitrogen. Step 3: Third CH2O Mannich addition extends chain to 5-carbon skeleton. Step 4: Intramolecular cyclization forms the pyrrolidine ring. Step 5: Reduction of imine/hemiaminal intermediates by H2. Net stoichiometry: Glycine (C2) + 3 CH2O (3×C1) + 2H2 → Proline (C5) + 3H2O. Montmorillonite concentrates reactants and templates the cyclization. Wet-dry cycling drives dehydration steps.
reactions.reasoning: The reaction of formaldehyde with glycine to produce proline, sarcosine, and betaine is documented experimentally under prebiotic conditions (Sources 2595). The stoichiometry requires 3 equivalents of CH2O to account for the C2→C5 carbon increase, plus 2 H2 as reductant to balance hydrogen (correcting the stoichiometry error noted in the previous attempt). Montmorillonite catalyzes polymerization and Mannich-type reactions (Sources 9431). This represents the most direct experimentally supported route to proline.

reactions.name: Succinaldehyde + ammonia cyclization to Δ1-pyrroline

conditions.other: OHC-CH2-CH2-CHO + NH3 → Δ1-pyrroline + H2O; imine formation + intramolecular cyclization

reactions.mechanism: NH3 attacks one aldehyde of succinaldehyde to form a hemiaminal, which dehydrates to an imine. The terminal amino group then attacks the second aldehyde intramolecularly to form the 5-membered ring, losing a second H2O. Net: C4H6O2 + NH3 → C4H7N + 2H2O. The 5-membered ring is thermodynamically stable.
reactions.reasoning: Intramolecular cyclization of dialdehydes with amines is a well-known reaction in organic chemistry. Formation of Δ1-pyrroline from succinaldehyde and ammonia is the basis of the Knorr pyrrole synthesis and related reactions. Under prebiotic conditions, this provides the cyclic imine scaffold for the proline aminonitrile route. Montmorillonite concentrates reactants and promotes dehydration.

reactions.name: HCN addition to Δ1-pyrroline: proline aminonitrile formation

conditions.other: Strecker-type: cyclic imine + HCN → α-aminonitrile (cyclic)

reactions.mechanism: CN- (from HCN deprotonation) attacks the electrophilic C=N carbon of Δ1-pyrroline. The nucleophilic addition forms pyrrolidine-2-carbonitrile (proline aminonitrile). This is a Strecker-type addition of cyanide to a cyclic imine, forming the C-CN bond that will become the carboxylate.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker addition of HCN to imines is the classic pathway for prebiotic aminonitrile formation (Strecker, 1850; Miller, 1953; Sources 21189, 10835). Sources 22185 specifically describe the unique behavior of proline aminonitrile under prebiotic conditions, indicating this intermediate has been experimentally characterized. The cyclic imine substrate provides direct access to the cyclic amino acid.

reactions.name: Proline aminonitrile hydrolysis to proline

conditions.other: C5H8N2 + 2H2O → C5H9NO2 + NH3; nitrile → amide → carboxylic acid

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group (-CN) of proline aminonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: CN + H2O → CONH2 (amide) + H2O → COOH (carboxylic acid) + NH3. The pyrrolidine ring is stable under these conditions. Product is racemic proline.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acid is a standard aqueous reaction that occurs under mildly acidic or basic conditions and at elevated temperatures. This is the final step of the Strecker synthesis. Sources 22185 discuss proline aminonitrile behavior, noting its unique properties compared to other aminonitriles. Hydrolysis completes the conversion to proline.

pathways.name: Full hydrothermal-to-surface Mannich route (CO2 origin)
pathways.description: Complete CO2-to-proline pathway via hydrothermal CO2 fixation to formate, reduction to formaldehyde, surface Strecker synthesis to glycine, then Mannich cyclization with 3 additional formaldehyde + H2 reductant to proline. This is the most experimentally supported route (Sources 2595).

pathways.name: HKG cycle biological-analog route (pyruvate + glyoxylate → α-KG → glutamate → P5C → proline)
pathways.description: Pyruvate (from hydrothermal CO2 fixation) and glyoxylate undergo aldol condensation (HKG cycle) to form α-KG (C3+C2=C5). α-KG is reductively aminated to glutamate, which is partially reduced to GSA, spontaneously cyclizes to P5C, and is reduced to proline. This mimics the biological proline biosynthesis pathway.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic glycolonitrile-to-glycine Mannich route
pathways.description: Formaldehyde + HCN → glycolonitrile → glycine (cyanosulfidic pathway), then glycine + 3 CH2O + 2H2 → proline via Mannich cyclization. This variant uses the glycolonitrile intermediate rather than direct Strecker synthesis.

pathways.name: Direct Strecker glycine to Mannich proline (surface-only short route)
pathways.description: Shortest route using pre-existing formaldehyde and HCN. Formaldehyde acts as both Strecker aldehyde for glycine synthesis and as Mannich reagent for ring closure. All reactions occur in surface evaporitic pool environment.

pathways.name: Proline aminonitrile route via hydrothermal α-KG decarboxylation
pathways.description: α-KG (from HKG cycle) undergoes thermal decarboxylation to succinic semialdehyde (C5→C4), which is reduced to succinaldehyde on Fe0, cyclizes with NH3 to Δ1-pyrroline, then Strecker addition of HCN gives proline aminonitrile, hydrolysis yields proline. This is the most chemically distinct pathway.

pathways.name: Acetate-to-pyruvate carboxylation pathway to proline via glutamate
pathways.description: CO2 → formate → acetate (Wood-Ljungdahl analog) → pyruvate (carboxylation) → α-KG (HKG cycle) → glutamate → GSA → P5C → proline. This route emphasizes the acetyl-CoA pathway analog for C2 production.

pathways.name: Formose-glyoxylate convergent pathway
pathways.description: Formaldehyde → glycolaldehyde (formose) → glyoxylate (UV photocatalysis on TiO2) → combines with pyruvate in HKG aldol → α-KG → glutamate → P5C → proline. This route generates glyoxylate from surface photochemistry rather than hydrothermal carboxylation.

pathways.name: Proline aminonitrile route via formose-photochemistry glyoxylate
pathways.description: Surface-origin glyoxylate (from glycolaldehyde UV oxidation) enters HKG cycle to make α-KG, which is decarboxylated and reduced to succinaldehyde, cyclized with NH3, and converted to proline via Strecker aminonitrile pathway. Combines surface photochemistry with hydrothermal reductive chemistry.

pathways.name: Direct CO2 fixation to pyruvate, then biological-analog proline
pathways.description: Most direct hydrothermal route: CO2 + H2 → pyruvate on greigite/magnetite (one-pot), then surface/biochemical HKG cycle steps to proline. Bypasses formate/formaldehyde intermediates. Pyruvate is the key hub.

pathways.name: Acetate-pyruvate proline aminonitrile convergent route
pathways.description: Combines Wood-Ljungdahl-type acetate synthesis (rxn_005) with carboxylation to pyruvate, HKG cycle to α-KG, then branches through the proline aminonitrile route (decarboxylation → succinaldehyde → Δ1-pyrroline → Strecker → proline). Maximum environmental diversity.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The final Mannich step (rxn_020) condenses a multi-step process (glycine + 3 HCHO + reductant) into a single reaction. While Sources 2595 mention proline formation from glycine + formaldehyde, the mechanism for assembling the pyrrolidine ring in one step is poorly characterized. The stoichiometry and ring-closure selectivity are unclear. Earlier steps are sound.
suggestions: Decompose rxn_020 into explicit stepwise Mannich additions and cyclization
Specify reductant identity and mineral catalyst for the Mannich cyclization
Consider montmorillonite or TiO2 surfaces for the formaldehyde condensation step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Elegant biological-analog pathway but rxn_017 (glutamate → glutamate-5-semialdehyde) is the critical bottleneck. Partial reduction of a carboxylate to an aldehyde requires either ATP+NADPH (biological) or very specific reductant/catalyst under prebiotic conditions. H2 alone is insufficient for this selective transformation. rxn_003 (formate carboxylation to glyoxylate) also has limited experimental support. The spontaneous GSA→P5C cyclization (rxn_018) is well-known and chemically sound.
suggestions: Replace rxn_017 with a thioester-mediated partial reduction pathway
Provide alternative glyoxylate source (e.g., UV photolysis of glycolaldehyde)
Specify a suitable prebiotic reductant for rxn_019 (e.g., FeS/H2S system)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Glycolonitrile formation (rxn_009) is well-established in cyanosulfidic chemistry. The glycine production via hydrolysis/amination is standard. However, same rxn_020 Mannich issue as P1. Also, the formaldehyde source for rxn_009 is not specified in the pathway, and the pathway is surface-only with no hydrothermal precursor steps explicitly included in the 4-step description.
suggestions: Include explicit formaldehyde sourcing step
Decompose Mannich cyclization

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Extremely short pathway (2 steps) is efficient but lacks environmental depth and sourcing of starting materials. Both steps are surface reactions with no hydrothermal contribution. The Mannich step remains problematic. The pathway implicitly assumes availability of formaldehyde, HCN, NH3 without tracing their origin.
suggestions: Extend pathway to include hydrothermal precursor generation
Decompose rxn_020 into explicit sub-steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The strongest pathway in the network. The final three steps (succinaldehyde→Δ1-pyrroline→proline aminonitrile→proline) are all well-supported: rxn_021 is a classic intramolecular condensation, rxn_022 is explicitly supported by Sources 22185 (proline aminonitrile formation), and rxn_023 is standard nitrile hydrolysis. The α-KG sourcing via HKG cycle (rxn_014/015) is supported by Sources 10738. Weakest links are rxn_003 (glyoxylate formation) and rxn_008 (selective reduction to succinaldehyde). Overall the best-justified route to the target.
suggestions: Provide alternative glyoxylate source via glycolaldehyde oxidation (rxn_012→rxn_013) to avoid rxn_003
Specify Fe0 nanoparticle conditions more precisely for rxn_008
Consider whether succinic semialdehyde→succinaldehyde could proceed via thioester intermediate

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Long pathway (10 steps) with multiple weak reactions. The acetate→pyruvate carboxylation (rxn_006) lacks strong experimental support under prebiotic conditions. Combined with the problematic rxn_017 (glutamate→GSA), this pathway has two critical bottlenecks. The acetyl-CoA pathway analog via formate (rxn_005) is interesting but the overall chain is fragile.
suggestions: Replace acetate→pyruvate with direct CO2 fixation (rxn_004)
Address rxn_017 bottleneck as suggested for P2

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Uses formose chemistry (rxn_012) and glycolaldehyde oxidation (rxn_013) as an alternative glyoxylate source, which is more plausible than direct formate carboxylation. However, still suffers from the rxn_017 bottleneck. The formose self-condensation requires careful catalyst control (e.g., borate minerals) and UV oxidation for rxn_013 should be specified.
suggestions: Specify borate or Ca(OH)2 catalyst for formose step
Specify UV or MnO2 oxidant for glycolaldehyde→glyoxylate
Address rxn_017 as in P2

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is essentially P5 with the glyoxylate sourced from formose/oxidation rather than direct carboxylation. This is a better glyoxylate route. The final aminonitrile steps remain strong. Long (11 steps) but each step is at least marginally plausible. A good alternative to P5.
suggestions: Specify formose catalyst
Consider combining with P5 as convergent variant

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Uses direct CO2→pyruvate (rxn_004) which is plausible but low-yielding. Combined with the rxn_017 glutamate→GSA bottleneck, the pathway is weakened. The direct CO2 fixation simplifies the early steps but doesn't resolve the core issue of the biological-analog reductive route.
suggestions: Bypass rxn_017 entirely by routing through aminonitrile pathway after α-KG

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Convergent route combining acetate→pyruvate with the aminonitrile terminal steps. The acetate carboxylation (rxn_006) is the weakest early link. The final aminonitrile sequence is the strongest part. An 11-step route with moderate overall plausibility.
suggestions: Replace acetate→pyruvate with direct CO2 fixation
Provide experimental support for rxn_006 conditions

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-designed and comprehensive network with excellent topology (23 nodes, 5 hubs, 6 convergence points, 10 pathways). The three complementary strategies (Mannich, biological-analog, aminonitrile) demonstrate creative thinking and good use of literature. The network correctly identifies key literature references (Sources 2595 for Mannich, Sources 10738 for HKG cycle, Sources 22185 for proline aminonitrile). Hub molecules (formaldehyde, glycine, pyruvate, α-KG, P5C) are well-chosen and create genuine convergence. Environmental flow (Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical) is well-structured with mineral catalysts specified for hydrothermal reactions. However, three critical chemical issues prevent approval: (1) rxn_020 (Mannich proline formation) is oversimplified—the mechanism for glycine + 3 HCHO → pyrrolidine ring needs decomposition into explicit sub-steps; (2) rxn_017 (glutamate → GSA) requires partial carboxylate→aldehyde reduction, which is extremely challenging without enzymatic machinery; (3) Several early carbon fixation steps (rxn_003, rxn_006) lack strong experimental support. The aminonitrile route (P5/P8) is the most promising, with the best-supported final steps (Δ1-pyrroline + HCN → proline aminonitrile → proline). Addressing rxn_017 by routing all biological-analog pathways through the aminonitrile branch after α-KG, and decomposing rxn_020 into explicit steps, would significantly strengthen the network.
